text: The patient is positioned laterally with the left side facing upwards and the right side downwards, preparing for the left adrenalectomy procedure.
The surgeon begins mobilizing the descending colon and the splenic flexure, likely using surgical instruments to carefully separate these structures from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon mobilizes the descending colon, ensuring that dissection does not extend behind the kidney.
The surgeon mobilizes the descending colon, revealing the medial portion of the inferior pole and the medial portion of the superior pole of the kidney.
The surgeon exposes the hilum, revealing the adrenal vein, with the surrounding anatomical structures visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the adrenal vein, which is located more medially in the surgical field.
The surgeon mobilizes tissues superiorly to expose the left crus of the diaphragm, revealing its anatomical position.
The surgeon encounters minor bleeding from the dissected tissue plane, with visible blood pooling in the surgical field.
The surgeon flips the spleen upward, revealing the pancreas along with the splenic vein and splenic artery, requiring careful manipulation to avoid damage.
The surgeon mobilizes the tissue, revealing the medial portion of the kidney, and palpates the area to assess the anatomical structures.
The kidney is prominently visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the pancreas, indicating that they are in the incorrect anatomical plane during the dissection.
The surgeon identifies the splenic vein and artery located superiorly in the surgical field.
The surgeon begins to open the Gerota's fascia, revealing the underlying structures.
The surgeon opens the tissue at the level of the hilum, focusing on the anatomical structures in this region.
The surgeon identifies the vein and carefully follows its course inferiorly, adjusting the dissection plane to maintain proximity to the vein.
The surgeon uses one hand to push the kidney upwards, stretching the surrounding tissues to improve visibility and access to the surgical area.
The surgeon identifies the artery, which is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon visually identifies the artery, which is positioned deeper than the vein within the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the vein, which is positioned higher and more superficial compared to the artery, in the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the renal vein, which is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon follows the renal vein inferiorly towards the inferior vena cava (IVC), ensuring the superior border is clean to locate the adrenal vein.
The surgeon removes the surrounding fat tissue, likely to improve visibility and access to underlying structures such as veins and arteries.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to remove surrounding fat tissue after identifying the adrenal vein.
The surgeon begins using scissors to perform a finer dissection, focusing on precise tissue separation.
The surgeon clips the small vessel before transecting it, ensuring secure closure prior to cutting.
The surgeon continues the mobilization of anatomical structures, indicating that the process is not yet complete.
The surgeon prepares to expose the left crus of the diaphragm, indicating an upcoming visual of this anatomical structure.
The left crus of the diaphragm is visible alongside the stomach, which is positioned nearby and appears to be encroaching into the surgical field.
The surgeon begins the dissection to separate the adrenal gland from the kidney, focusing on the anatomical connection between these structures.
The Vesta sealer is used to complete the dissection, sealing and dividing tissue planes as it progresses.
The surgeon visually identifies the kidney and follows its outline, preparing for further dissection or manipulation.
The surgeon carefully removes the adrenal gland from the kidney, ensuring no visible bleeding occurs during the separation process.
Blood is visible as the surgeon navigates through the adrenal gland, indicating potential contact or dissection of the gland tissue.
The surgeon removes a fibrous fatty layer from the surface of the kidney and diaphragm, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.
